content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

March 24, 1983


Honorable Willie Brown
Speaker of the Assembly
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA 95814

Our Advice No. A-83-076

Dear Mr. Speaker:

This letter is in response to your telephone request for
advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b).1/ You
have asked whether Section 87100 of the Political Reform Act
requires you to disqualify yourself from voting on,
participating in the consideration of, or using your official
position to influence, Senate Bill 259. Our conclusion, which
is based on the facts you have given us and the legal analysis
set forth below, is that disqualification is required in this
instance.

FACTS

Richard Traweek, president and owner of Traweek Investments,
made a gift of over $250 to you within the past 12 months.
Traweek owns the John Muir Apartments, a 720 unit rental complex
in San Francisco. Traweek wants to convert these rental units
to condominiums.

Prior to December 1982, San Francisco had an ordinance
limiting the number of rental units which could be converted to
condominiums to 1,000 per year. The City made a priority
approval list each year of apartment owners who wanted to
convert rental units to condominiums. Only 1,000 rental units
were added to the list each year. Apartment owners paid to
register rental units on this list. Once on the list, an
apartment owner was entitled to prepare and file the necessary

1/ All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise indicated.

Honorable Willie Brown
March 24, 1983
Page 2


applications for conversion of his registered rental units.
However, being on this list did not guarantee approval of the
condominium conversion application.

In 1982, Traweek secured the last 187 places on the priority
registration list for units in the John Muir complex. During
1982, Traweek spent $300,000 in preliminary processing for the
condominium conversion applications for the 187 registered
units. In December 1982, the San Francisco Board of Supervisors
amended the condominium conversion ordinance to limit the total
number of conversions in the City to 200, and to ban all
conversions in buildings with more than seven rental units.
Under the new ordinance, no condominium conversions will be
allowed for the 187 John Muir rental units registered by Traweek
in 1982.

Legislation has been introduced (SB 259) in response to
San Francisco's new ordinance on condominium conversion. The
legislation affects those cities or counties which have
established both a quota for condominium conversions and a
registration system, like San Francisco's, requiring an apart-
ment owner to pay a fee for placement on a limited priority list
before being permitted to file an application for a condominium
conversion. If such a city or county were to reduce its yearly
quota for condominium conversions, so that an apartment owner on
the priority list could not file an application to convert a
previously registered apartment unit to a condominium, the
legislation would require the city or county to allow the owner
to file conversion applications immediately for all apartment
units in any complex for which any units had been registered
under the old quota system.

There are six cities or counties, other than San Francisco,
which have quotas and registration ordinances for condominium
conversions -- Petaluma, Davis, Fontana, Morgan Hill, Santa Cruz
and Santa Cruz County. These are the only jurisdictions which
could potentially be affected by the legislation. The legis-
lation would not affect them, however, unless they were also to
change their ordinances, reducing the quota of condominium
conversions so that owners with registered units were deprived
of the opportunity to file conversion applications. So far as
we know, this has not occurred in any of the six other
jurisdictions.

Honorable Willie Brown
March 24, 1983
Page 3


ANALYSIS

Government Code Section 87100 applies to state legislators.
Section 87100 states that "no public official" shall make or
participate in decisions in which he or she has a financial
interest.2/ A legislator is a public official within the
meaning of Section 87100. Sections 82048, 82049.

A public official has a financial interest in a decision,
and thus must disqualify himself, if the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, upon an entity which has been a source of
income or gifts of $250 or more to him within the past 12
months. Section 87103.3/ During the past 12 months, Traweek
has been a source of income to you, as defined by Section
87030(a), of over $250. From the facts given, it is reasonably
foreseeable that the effect of the legislation on Traweek - to
permit Traweek to process condominium conversion applications
for all 720 units in the John Muir complex -- would be material.4/
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702. Thus, unless the effect of the
legislation on Traweek is indistinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, you must disqualify yourself.

For the "public generally" exception to apply to this
situation, the legislation would have to apply to a "significant
segment" of the public, and it would have to affect Traweek "in
substantially the same manner" as the other members of that

2/ However, the Political Reform Act provides no remedies
for violations of Section 87100 by state legislators. Section
87102.

3/ You have also received campaign contributions from
Traweek. However, Section 87100 does not require disqualification based on campaign contributions. Section 84308, a
newly enacted section of the Political Reform Act which requires
certain public officials to disqualify themselves from matters
affecting their contributors, does not apply to state
legislators.

4/ The gross receipts for the sale of 720 condominium
units would clearly exceed $100,000. Even the cost of filing
applications for 720 units would probably exceed $100,000. The
inability to sell 187 units would undoubtedly cost Traweek more
than $100,00 in lost revenues. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702(b)(3)(C).

Honorable Willie Brown
March 24, 1983
Page 4


segment of the public. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703. From
the facts we have been given, we cannot say that either aspect
of this test is satisfied.

In the case of a state elected officer, such as a state
legislator, an entire industry, trade or profession constitutes
a significant segment of the public generally. 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18703(a). However, SB 259 would not affect an entire
industry, but only a small part of an industry. It would affect
owners of rental units in at most seven jurisdictions in the
state, and, within those jurisdictions, only those owners who
have registered rental units on a priority list for condominium
conversions. The proposed legislation would not affect all (or
even almost all) members of the industry, i.e., owners of rental
units throughout the state, and thus Section 18703(a) does not
apply.

Section 18703(a) aside, it is clear that Traweek, as an
owner of over 700 rental housing units, would not be considered
to be part of a "significant segment" of the public. In the
Ferraro Opinion, 4 FPPC 62 (1978) the Commission ruled that
owners of four or more rental units within a jurisdiction do not
constitute a significant segment of the public. It follows that
owners of four or more rental housing units, such as Traweek, in
only seven jurisdictions cannot constitute a significant segment
of the public generally in the entire State of California.5/

Even if owners of rental units were in the seven jurisdic-
tions held to be a significant segment of the public, the second
half of the test, that Traweek be affected "in substantially the
same manner" as other owners of rental units, is not satisfied.
It appears from the facts that currently, only those owners with
rental units already registered on the priority list in
San Francisco would be affected by the legislation. Those
owners would not all be affected to the same degree as Traweek
which, as a result of the ordinance, would be able to process
applications not only for its 187 registered units, but for all
720 units in the John Muir complex. Thus it appears that even
within the segment of the public potentially affected by the
legislation, the effect on Traweek would be readily
distinguishable.

On the basis of the foregoing analysis, it appears
reasonable foreseeable that SB 259 would have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public

_______________________
5/ The jurisdiction of a state legislator is the entire
state. Section 82035.

Honorable Willie Brown
March 24, 1983
Page 5


generally, upon Traweek. Therefore, you are required to
disqualify yourself under Section 87100 from voting on, from
participating in any discussion or consideration of, or from
using your official position to influence SB 259.

It should be noted that, although disqualification is
required under Section 87100, disqualification might not be
necessary under the legislative Ethics Code, Section 8920,
et seq.6/ However, Section 8920 in no way preempts Section
87100. The State Constitution specifically authorizes both the
Legislature and the people, by way of the initiative process, to
enact legislation governing conflicts of interest by State
legislators. Cal. Const. Article IV, Section 5. The legislature
exercised its powers under this section by enacting the
Legislative Code of Ethics. The people exercised their powers
by initiative through the adoption of the Political Reform Act.
In the case of a conflict between the two statutes, the
Political Reform Act would prevail over Section 8920, since
Section 81013 specifically provides that whenever the provisions
of the Political Reform Act conflict with any act of the
Legislature, the provisions of the Political Reform Act shall
prevail. See also Cal. Const. Article IV, Section 10(c).

I hope this letter has answered your questions about
SB 259. If you have any further questions about the Political
Reform Act, please do not hesitate to call us.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief, Legal Division

BAM:km

6/ Section 8920(b)(5) provides that a legislator may have
disqualify himself from voting on legislation in which he has
a personal interest. Section 8922(a) provides that a legis-
lator does not have a personal interest in legislation if his
relationship to any potential beneficiary of the legislation is
one defined as a "remote interest" in Section 1091. Speaker
Brown's relationship to Traweek is his receipt of a gift of over
$250. There are no cases which have held that receipt of a gift
is a disqualifying interest under either Section 8920, et seq.,
or Section 1090, et seq. Of course, for a definitive
interpretation of Section 8920, et seq., Legislative Counsel,
not the FPPC, should be consulted.

sections.question_synthetic: Must Speaker Willie Brown disqualify himself from voting on Senate Bill 259 under Political Reform Act Section 87100, given that he received a gift of over $250 from Richard Traweek, who would be materially benefited by the legislation?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Disqualification is required under Section 87100 because Traweek, a source of gifts over $250 to Speaker Brown in the past 12 months, would receive a material financial effect from SB 259 that is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. The legislation affects only a small segment of the rental unit industry in seven California jurisdictions and would benefit Traweek differently than other similarly situated owners.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear QUESTION, FACTS, ANALYSIS, and CONCLUSION sections. Document is FPPC response to Speaker Brown's request. All key sections extracted verbatim. Minor confidence reduction due to some ambiguity in footnote content and hypothetical nature of 'public generally' analysis.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked whether Section 87100 of the Political Reform Act requires you to disqualify yourself from voting on, participating in the consideration of, or using your official position to influence, Senate Bill 259.

CONCLUSION: On the basis of the foregoing analysis, it appears reasonably foreseeable that SB 259 would have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, upon Traweek. Therefore, you are required to disqualify yourself under section 87100 from voting on, from participating in any discussion or consideration of, or from using your official position to influence SB 259.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter A-83-076 advises Speaker Willie Brown that he must disqualify himself from voting on Senate Bill 259 because the legislation would have a material financial effect on Richard Traweek, a gift donor, that is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, triggering Political Reform Act Section 87100 disqualification requirements. The legislation addresses San Francisco's new condominium conversion restrictions that would specifically benefit Traweek's John Muir Apartments complex.
